perturbation.original_text: All documents, letters, reports, protocols, notes, journals, drawings, plans, maps, sketches, spreadsheets, data, and any other written material, correspondence, archive, or e-mail (including any electronic material) containing confidential information, as well as any copies of these materials, must be returned by the potential Franchisee upon written request to do so from the Franchisor or at any time at the Franchisor’s discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents, letters, reports, protocols, notes, journals, drawings, plans, maps, sketches, spreadsheets, data, and any other written material, correspondence, archive, or e-mail (including any electronic material) containing confidential information, as well as any copies of these materials, may be returned by the potential Franchisee at the Franchisor’s discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "must be returned" to "may be returned" significantly weakens the obligation to return confidential information. Furthermore, removing "upon written request to do so from the Franchisor or at any time" removes the Franchisor's explicit right to demand the return of sensitive materials, potentially conflicting with legal duties to protect trade secrets under New Brunswick law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act protects businesses from the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the requirement for the potential franchisee to return confidential information, the contract makes it more difficult for the franchisor to enforce their rights under the Act if the franchisee were to misappropriate trade secrets. While the Act doesn't mandate a specific clause, the weakening of return obligations undermines the franchisor's ability to take 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets, a key element in establishing a claim under the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the return of confidential information, while the changed text makes it optional ("may be returned"). This directly undermines the franchisor's ability to protect its trade secrets, as the franchisee is no longer obligated to return sensitive materials upon request. The law explanation indicates that this weakening hinders the franchisor's ability to take 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets, a key element in establishing a claim under the Trade Secrets Act. The scraped snippets are not useful in this case.

perturbation.original_text: Furthermore, the potential Franchisee agrees to not discuss or communicate any aspect whatsoever of the confidential information, be it directly or indirectly, to any agent, director, employee, or any other representative EXCEPT if these individuals (i) have been informed of the confidential nature of the information and (ii) have duly completed and signed the certificate presented in Annex “A”.
perturbation.changed_text: Furthermore, the potential Franchisee agrees to not discuss or communicate any aspect whatsoever of the confidential information, be it directly or indirectly, to any agent, director, employee, or any other representative.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception clause, the modification places an absolute restriction on disclosing confidential information, even to those who are informed and have signed a confidentiality agreement. This could be problematic because it may hinder the potential Franchisee's ability to consult with legal counsel or other necessary advisors during the evaluation process. This could violate implicit rights to seek legal advice, even under confidentiality, potentially conflicting with basic legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Contract Law (common law principle applicable in New Brunswick)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statutory law, the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing is a recognized common law principle in New Brunswick and other Canadian jurisdictions. This duty requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. By imposing an absolute restriction on disclosing confidential information, even to advisors who are bound by confidentiality, the modified clause could be seen as a violation of this implied duty, as it prevents the potential franchisee from obtaining necessary advice and guidance during the negotiation process. This change makes it difficult for the potential franchisee to evaluate the information provided, and therefore act with full knowledge.

perturbation.justification: The original text allowed disclosure of confidential information to agents, directors, or employees under specific conditions (informed consent and signed certificate). The changed text removes this exception, creating an absolute restriction on disclosure. The Law Explanation indicates this could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, hindering the franchisee's ability to obtain necessary advice. The change can prevent the potential franchisee from fully evaluating the information. Although the provided snippets do not directly address this issue, the explanation highlights a possible conflict with established legal principles of fair dealing by obstructing access to legal counsel. Based on the Perturbation Definition for Omission Legal Contradiction, removing essential information (the exception clause) creates a legal loophole because it removes a legally mandated consumer protection (the ability to seek legal advice.)

perturbation.original_text: The potential Franchisee agrees to take the necessary measures to ensure that the provisions of the present Agreement are respected to the same or greater degree that they would reasonably expect the confidentiality of their own information of the same nature to be respected.
perturbation.changed_text: The potential Franchisee agrees to take reasonable measures to ensure that the provisions of the present Agreement are respected.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the comparative clause weakens the standard of care required by the potential Franchisee. The original text requires them to protect the Franchisor's information with the same or greater diligence as they would their own. The revised text simply requires 'reasonable measures', which is a lower standard that could violate trade secret protections as it lowers the duty of care expected from the potential franchisee.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act requires the trade secret holder to undertake steps to protect it from leakage. By lowering the standard of care expected of the potential franchisee in protecting the confidential information, the alteration makes it more difficult for the franchisor to demonstrate they took reasonable measures to protect the trade secret if it were misappropriated.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires a higher standard of care to protect confidential information than the altered contract. The original text mandates the franchisee to protect the franchisor's information at least as well as they protect their own, while the altered text only requires "reasonable measures." The Law Explanation supports that the Trade Secrets Act necessitates the trade secret holder take steps to protect the trade secret from leakage. Reducing the standard of care expected of the potential franchisee in protecting the confidential information weakens the franchisor's ability to demonstrate they took reasonable measures to protect the trade secret if it were misappropriated. Therefore, by reducing the standard of care, the changed text creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any copies of the materials that may have been made must be destroyed (subject to applicable laws and in consideration of requirements for internal auditing, in which case the provisions of the present Agreement will continue to be applied to the confidential information which remains to be processed) in compliance with the procedure determined by the Franchisor concerning the destruction of similar confidential material.
perturbation.changed_text: Any copies of the materials that may have been made may be destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'must be destroyed' to 'may be destroyed' and removing the section in parenthesis along with 'in compliance with the procedure determined by the Franchisor concerning the destruction of similar confidential material.' significantly weakens the obligation to destroy confidential information and removes the Franchisor's control over the process. This can violate compliance laws if the information is not destroyed after a prescribed period and can have privacy implications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information and to destroy it when it is no longer needed for the identified purposes. By weakening the destruction requirement for confidential materials, the contract increases the risk of non-compliance with PIPEDA if those materials contain personal information. The original text provided a stronger guarantee that confidential information would be destroyed, mitigating this risk; removing this text weakens the protection of privacy.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the destruction of confidential material, while the changed text makes it optional ('may be destroyed'). This weakens the obligation to protect personal information as described in the Law Explanation regarding PIPEDA compliance. The scraped snippet 2, though brief, mentions PIPEDA's requirements, and the Law Explanation specifies PIPEDA requires organizations to destroy personal information when it is no longer needed. By making destruction optional, the revised contract increases the risk of non-compliance with PIPEDA. This constitutes a legal contradiction because the original text provided a stronger guarantee that confidential information would be destroyed mitigating this risk, which the revised text weakens.
